authoritative_sources.title: Guidelines for Securing Wireless Local Area Networks
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes U.S. federal security guidance, and SP 800-153 directly addresses WLAN security planning, deployment, operation, maintenance, and secure-use lifecycle expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: wireless security planning, access prerequisite validation, operational readiness, monitoring, and maintenance
practical_use_in_pack: access-control and WLAN operations evidence requirements
security caveats for guest, enterprise, and tenant-specific deployments

authoritative_sources.title: IEEE 802.11 Wireless LAN Working Group
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IEEE 802.11 is the primary standards venue for wireless LAN MAC and PHY behavior, amendments, and terminology that constrain claims about RF, association, roaming, client capabilities, and 2.4/5/6 GHz operation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: RF and WLAN technology baseline, association and roaming terminology, capacity and capability review

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 3748 Extensible Authentication Protocol
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IETF RFC defines EAP behavior used by enterprise WLAN 802.1X authentication flows, making it directly authoritative for access-intake, prerequisite, and authentication evidence guidance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: secure access intake, EAP evidence review, 802.1X troubleshooting

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 2865 Remote Authentication Dial In User Service (RADIUS)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IETF RFC defines RADIUS message and attribute behavior used by WLAN authentication and authorization paths, so it governs evidence requirements for RADIUS/NAC integration recommendations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: RADIUS/NAC integration review, secure access authorization, authentication-failure triage

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 2131 Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IETF DHCPv4 standard defines lease and renewal behavior used for WLAN client onboarding, address pool sizing, relay diagnostics, and lease-exhaustion troubleshooting.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: client address intake, DHCP troubleshooting, lease-exhaustion and duplicate-address failure handling
practical_use_in_pack: routing to DNS/DHCP/IPAM when ownership shifts

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 8415 Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol for IPv6 (DHCPv6)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IETF DHCPv6 standard defines IPv6 configuration behavior needed for dual-stack WLAN guidance, DHCPv6 option evidence, and staged migration checks.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: IPv6 prerequisite validation, dual-stack migration, DHCPv6 failure handling

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 4861 Neighbor Discovery for IP version 6
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IETF Neighbor Discovery standard defines router discovery, neighbor reachability, and related IPv6 behavior that materially affects WLAN IPv6 roaming and reachability diagnostics.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: IPv6 reachability triage, router advertisement review, roaming edge diagnostics
practical_use_in_pack: neighbor discovery and RA evidence prompts

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 4862 IPv6 Stateless Address Autoconfiguration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The IETF SLAAC standard defines IPv6 autoconfiguration behavior that constrains WLAN dual-stack client addressing recommendations and migration troubleshooting.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: SLAAC validation, client-addressing review, IPv6 dual-stack migration

authoritative_sources.title: Cisco Catalyst 9800 Series Wireless Controller: Radio Frequency Reference Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Cisco is the controller vendor; its RF reference guide defines platform RF profiles, radio resource management, and operational evidence for Cisco Catalyst 9800 wireless deployments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: RF design, channel/power review, controller evidence intake, capacity and interference troubleshooting

authoritative_sources.title: Cisco Catalyst 9800 Series Wireless Controller Software Configuration Guide: WLAN Security
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Cisco publishes this official controller guide for WLAN security modes, AAA/RADIUS integration, guest access, and platform-specific configuration evidence used in secure onboarding guidance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: secure WLAN access intake, WLAN security review, AAA integration, guest isolation evidence
practical_use_in_pack: RADIUS/NAC and guest access evidence requirements

authoritative_sources.title: Wireless Mesh Networking
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Cisco Meraki is the platform publisher; this documentation defines managed wireless mesh gateway/repeater behavior and practical operational limits for Meraki deployments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: mesh/backhaul design, remote-office fallback review, gateway and repeater failure handling

authoritative_sources.title: Unlicensed Use of the 6 GHz Band; Expanding Flexible Use in Mid-Band Spectrum
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FCC is the U.S. spectrum regulator; its 6 GHz orders and rulemaking record constrain U.S. Wi-Fi 6E/7 band, power, indoor/outdoor, AFC, and geofencing assumptions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: 6 GHz regulatory prerequisite validation, spectrum caveat handling, low-confidence escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 is a recognized public control catalog for access control, audit and accountability, monitoring, configuration management, and risk controls relevant to WLAN evidence packets and refusal boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: evidence control mapping, access-control review, monitoring and audit trail, exception handling

authoritative_sources.title: Current committed package baseline for wireless-networking-specialist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Current package task baseline from wireless-networking-specialist
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Repository-owned baseline defines prior supported tasks, inputs, output shapes, and refusal rules that the refreshed spec pack preserves where still valid and supersedes as canonical source of truth.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: legacy boundary comparison, task preservation, stale assumption replacement
practical_use_in_pack: legacy scenario minimum retained and exceeded
refusal rules refined into spec-first boundaries

workflow_stages.name: Wireless request intake and scope normalization
workflow_stages.description: Collect request objective, desired output, tenant/site identity, affected SSIDs/APs/controllers, floor or site scope, client population, time range, production-impact intent, and business impact before analysis begins.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite, eligibility, and authorization validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate RF survey, AP/controller inventory, SSID/VLAN/subnet map, authentication path, DHCP/DNS/IPv6 records, monitoring telemetry, incident/change context, regulatory region, and approval or rollback owners.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Acceptance, refusal, or pre-execution routing
workflow_stages.description: Accept in-scope wireless advisory work, block missing prerequisites, refuse prohibited execution, and route endpoint, identity, application, firewall, WAN/BGP, DNS/IPAM-primary, legal, procurement, compliance, or safety-primary work before execution begins.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Source-grounded wireless execution guidance
workflow_stages.description: Produce RF, SSID, AP/controller, roaming, capacity, secure access, DHCP/DNS/IPv6, mesh/backhaul, troubleshooting, and readiness recommendations tied to public sources and tenant evidence.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Safety, spectrum, policy, and low-confidence escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate when regulatory region, 6 GHz power/AFC/geofencing facts, healthcare or safety-critical client needs, legal/compliance interpretation, conflicting vendor guidance, or approval authority is missing or unresolved.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Evidence packaging and closure
workflow_stages.description: Package source IDs, tenant evidence, assumptions, confidence, success criteria, rollback gates, monitoring gates, next owners, and commercialization-readiness notes while keeping tenant execution records tenant-scoped.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: request scope, tenant/site identity, affected SSIDs, APs, controllers, client classes, time range, business impact, and desired output
RF survey, predictive design, floor plan, AP placement map, antenna model, channel plan, transmit power plan, band policy, and capacity model
wireless controller or cloud management exports, AP inventory, firmware matrix, SSID/WLAN configuration summary, RF profile, radio event logs, and wireless health records
association, disassociation, roaming, authentication, EAP, RADIUS, guest/captive portal, DHCP, DNS, IPv6 RA/SLAAC/DHCPv6, gateway, firewall handoff, and flow telemetry
client class inventory, voice/video quality reports, roaming reports, packet loss, jitter, latency, throughput samples, mesh/backhaul health, and change or incident tickets
security posture, guest isolation, VLAN policy, NAC/RADIUS owner records, certificate dependency notes, regulatory region notes, exception or waiver records, and safety caveats
deployment plan, change window, approval owner, rollback checklist, monitoring gate, acceptance test, evidence packet, and commercialization readiness note
systems_of_record: wireless controller or cloud management platform
floor-plan, CAD, BIM, and site acceptance repositories
RADIUS, NAC, certificate, and network authentication log systems
DHCP, DNS, IPAM, IPv6 router advertisement, gateway, firewall handoff, and network monitoring systems
SIEM, logging, flow telemetry, packet capture, and wireless health dashboards
incident tickets, change tickets, runbooks, CMDB, service catalog, architecture decision records, and evidence repositories
policy, exception, risk, procurement/vendor commitment, legal/regulatory, safety, and approval systems relevant to wireless deployment

allowed: Accept, block, refuse, or route wireless-networking intake based on scope, prerequisites, evidence, tenant isolation, and authority.
Recommend SSID, RF/channel, AP/controller, segmentation, roaming, client capacity, secure access, DHCP/DNS/IPv6, monitoring, and rollback-readiness guidance.
Produce troubleshooting plans for association, authentication, DHCP/DNS, IPv6, roaming, RF interference, throughput, mesh/backhaul, and controller failover faults.
Prepare evidence-backed recommendations, intake disposition notes, prerequisite checklists, routing summaries, deployment evidence notes, and commercialization proof points.
Recommend handoff criteria to endpoint, identity, application, firewall, WAN/BGP, DNS/IPAM, legal, procurement, compliance, safety, or human reviewer owners.
escalate: Required RF survey, AP/controller inventory, SSID/VLAN/subnet map, client class, authentication path, DHCP/DNS/IPv6 records, monitoring telemetry, time range, or approval owner is missing.
Regulatory region, 6 GHz power class, AFC/geofencing, DFS/outdoor, safety-critical client, legal/compliance, procurement, or production approval facts are missing or conflicting.
Official standard, vendor guidance, tenant policy, contractual requirement, safety requirement, or jurisdictional rule conflicts without source precedence.
The answer depends on endpoint, identity, application, firewall, WAN/BGP, DNS/IPAM-primary, security operations, legal, procurement, compliance, safety, or execution expertise as primary owner.
Evidence is stale, contradictory, vendor-proprietary, inaccessible, cross-tenant, or below confidence threshold.
refuse: Endpoint OS hardening, endpoint MDM workflows, endpoint driver deployment, or device management platform policy details.
Identity/IAM platform design beyond WLAN authentication integration evidence and handoff requirements.
Application code, UI behavior, API feature logic, database query logic, or application-only performance debugging.
Full firewall policy migration, WAN/BGP configuration, DNS/IPAM-primary redesign, or direct vendor command scripting outside wireless operations.
Legal, regulatory, spectrum certification, compliance signoff, procurement, financial, safety, production change approval, or credentialed execution authority.

domain_failure_modes: RF survey or floor-plan absence makes AP density, channel, power, roaming, and 6 GHz guidance speculative.
Controller, AP, firmware, or cloud-management evidence is missing, stale, or vendor-specific but generalized across platforms.
Client capability, voice/video sensitivity, roaming behavior, or density assumptions are unknown and could invert the design tradeoff.
RADIUS/EAP, certificate, NAC, or guest admission evidence is incomplete, causing unsafe identity or access claims.
DHCP scope, relay, DNS resolver, IPv6 RA/SLAAC/DHCPv6, VLAN, or gateway records conflict, causing misdiagnosed WLAN client failures.
6 GHz, DFS, AFC, geofencing, outdoor operation, or regulatory-region assumptions are missing and materially affect legality or performance.
Wireless health telemetry appears complete but lacks packet loss, jitter, roaming, authentication, DHCP/DNS, or client-class context needed for root-cause confidence.
Mesh/backhaul or controller failover expectations are overstated without power, line-of-sight, uplink, gateway/repeater, and session-retention evidence.
Tenant safety, privacy, contractual, regulated-data, or retention policy materially changes the answer and requires human or adjacent authority.
Generic networking framing causes the lane to absorb firewall, WAN/BGP, DNS/IPAM, endpoint, identity, or application ownership instead of routing.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

required_workflow_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Wireless controller or cloud management records, RF surveys, AP inventory, WLAN/SSID configuration, RADIUS/NAC logs, DHCP/DNS/IPAM records, IPv6 RA/SLAAC/DHCPv6 evidence, monitoring telemetry, tickets, change records, policy repositories, and approval systems govern safe wireless advice.

required_workflow_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
required_workflow_questions.answer: RF physics, client roaming, airtime contention, 6 GHz spectrum constraints, EAP/RADIUS admission, DHCP/DNS/IPv6 client-edge behavior, mesh backhaul, controller failover, and site-specific safety constraints make wireless materially different from generic networking advice.

required_workflow_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Endpoint MDM, IAM design, application code, firewall migration, WAN/BGP policy, DNS/IPAM-primary design, legal/compliance signoff, procurement approval, safety acceptance, and production execution are adjacent or human-owner boundaries.

unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant client mix, controller vendor documentation, RF survey evidence, and observed telemetry before making vendor-specific claims.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific spectrum rules outside the United States
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Use FCC source only for U.S. examples; require tenant regulatory region and local authority source before jurisdiction-specific 6 GHz, DFS, AFC, outdoor, or power guidance.
Escalate to human reviewer or tenant authority when safety-critical clients, healthcare devices, legal retention, privacy, or contractual evidence rules materially change the answer.
